file_name: Kadirappa et al. - 2023 - An automated multi-class skin lesion diagnosis by embedding local and global features of Dermoscopy.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning methods, specifically a novel U-Net segmentation network with Spatial Attention Blocks (SASegNet) for skin lesion segmentation and EfficientNet B1 for multi-class skin lesion classification, to analyze dermoscopy images from ISIC and PH2 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel U-Net segmentation network (SASegNet) and an EfficientNet B1 classification model for multi-class skin lesion diagnosis. It details specific methodologies, datasets (ISIC, PH2), hyperparameters, and reports new quantitative results (e.g., AUC-ROC scores of 0.974, 0.972, etc.), distinguishing it from a review or survey.

title: An automated multi-class skin lesion diagnosis by embedding local and global features of Dermoscopy images
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated multi-class skin lesion diagnosis system that combines a novel U-Net segmentation network with Spatial Attention Blocks (SASegNet) and an EfficientNet B1 classifier. The SASegNet model accurately segments skin lesions to extract local features, which are fused with global features from original images to improve classification performance. The methodology was evaluated on the ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020 datasets for classification, and the PH2 dataset for segmentation. The proposed approach achieved high Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC) scores of 0.974, 0.972, 0.962, and 0.937 on the respective ISIC datasets. Additionally, the segmentation model attained 95% accuracy on the PH2 dataset. The results indicate that fusing local and global features enhances robustness against occlusions and inter-class similarities, aiding dermatologists in accurate diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Binary segmentation masks of skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes SASegNet, a U-Net based network with Spatial Attention Blocks, to segment skin lesions from dermoscopy images. It reports an accuracy of 95% and IoU/Dice scores on the PH2 dataset.

The paper performs multi-class skin lesion diagnosis using EfficientNet B1. It classifies images into binary classes (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) on ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2020, seven classes on ISIC-2018, and eight classes on ISIC-2019. It reports AUC-ROC scores of 0.974, 0.972, 0.962, and 0.937 for these datasets respectively.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopy image dataset containing ground truth binary segmentation masks, used for segmentation tasks.

use.training: Used for segmentation model training (stopped at 63 epochs).
use.validation: Used for segmentation model validation (loss monitored).
use.testing: Used for segmentation model testing and evaluation (IoU/Dice scores).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2017
datasets_used.description: A binary classification dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, also referred to as the ISBI 2017 challenge dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2018 (HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A highly imbalanced multi-class skin cancer dataset, commonly referred to as HAM10000.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A combination of ISIC2017, HAM10000, and BCN 20000 datasets, containing eight classes of skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2020
datasets_used.description: A binary classification dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration.

statistical_methods_used.method: AUC-ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the classification performance of the proposed model on ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020 datasets.
To derive classification metrics including Accuracy, Precision, Recall (Sensitivity), Specificity, and F1-Score.

statistical_methods_used.method: Dice Coefficient and Intersection over Union (IoU)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the segmentation performance of the SASegNet model on the PH2 dataset.
To assess the contribution of specific components (e.g., Spatial Attention Blocks) by comparing model performance with and without them.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics such as AUC-ROC, Accuracy, Specificity, Sensitivity, Precision, F1-Score, IoU, and Dice Coefficient, without statistical significance testing.

PH2.notes: Training stopped at 63 epochs due to no further improvement in validation loss. The proposed SASegNet achieved 95% accuracy as stated in the abstract, but detailed metrics in Table 6 show 96.2% accuracy.

ISIC-2017.notes: The proposed methodology (EfficientNet B1 with multiscale fusion) achieved the highest AUC-ROC of 0.974.

ISIC-2018 (HAM10000).notes: For melanoma vs non-melanoma prediction specifically, the positive prediction rate improved from 0.91 vs 0.97 (without fusion) to 0.907 vs 0.975 (with fusion) for B0 and B1 respectively.

ISIC-2019.notes: For melanoma vs non-melanoma prediction specifically, the true positive precision rate improved from 0.896 vs 0.948 (without fusion) to 0.937 vs 0.973 (with fusion) for B0 and B1 respectively.

ISIC-2020.notes: No specific per-class breakdown provided for ISIC-2020 beyond aggregate metrics.

architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNet B1 was pre-trained on ImageNet (1000 class classification) and used for transfer learning; base model weights were frozen.

segmentation_model.name: SASegNet
segmentation_model.description: Novel U-Net segmentation network with Spatial Attention Blocks to emphasize specific regions.

classification_model.name: EfficientNet B1
classification_model.structure: Built using five modules and three subblocks; modules 1 & 2 use depthwise convolution, modules 3, 4 & 5 use conventional convolution; Batch Normalization used in all modules except module 3; skip connections form sub-blocks.
classification_model.feature_extraction: Local features from SASegNet segmentation results and global features from original pre-processed images are concatenated.

dense_layers.neurons: Variable based on dataset classes (2 for ISIC-2017/2020, 7 for ISIC-2018, 8 for ISIC-2019)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 256 × 256 pixels to handle non-uniform resolution and maintain input uniformity
Segmentation using SASegNet to extract lesion regions and remove background artifacts (hairs, rulers, markers)
Batch normalization applied to feature maps in the segmentation network
Spatial Dropout used in contracting and expanding stages of the segmentation network to regularize activations
Data augmentation including Flip, Translate, and Rotate during training
conclusions: The proposed SASegNet segmentation network with Spatial Attention Blocks achieved 95% accuracy on the PH2 dataset, outperforming baseline U-Net models.

Fusing local features from segmentation results with global features from original images using EfficientNet B1 significantly improved classification performance compared to using global features alone.
The proposed methodology achieved benchmark AUC-ROC scores of 0.974, 0.972, 0.962, and 0.937 on the ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, ISIC-2019, and ISIC-2020 datasets, respectively.
The model demonstrated robustness against inter-class similarities (e.g., between Melanocytic Nevi and Dermatofibroma) and occlusions such as hairs, rulers, and gel bubbles.
The automated diagnosis system is capable of aiding practicing dermatologists in achieving robust and accurate multi-class skin lesion diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies on retrospective public datasets (ISIC-2017/2018/2019/2020 and PH2) without external clinical validation or prospective testing, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.

The ISIC-2018 and ISIC-2019 datasets exhibit severe class imbalance, with Melanocytic Nevi (NV) comprising 50.82% of ISIC-2019, while Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular lesions (VASC) are severely underrepresented (1.85% and 1.96% respectively), potentially biasing model performance.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline models.
The paper claims 'benchmark results' without providing rigorous statistical comparisons or confidence intervals for the reported AUC-ROC scores.

The segmentation model training was stopped at 63 epochs due to lack of validation loss improvement, which may indicate early stopping rather than full convergence, though this is a standard regularization technique.

The methodology uses minimal preprocessing (resizing and segmentation) to remove artifacts, but the robustness of the segmentation step (SASegNet) is critical; errors in segmentation could propagate to the classification stage, though this risk is not explicitly quantified.

The PH2 dataset used for segmentation validation is small (200 images) and contains only three classes (Normal Nevi, Atypical Nevi, Melanoma), limiting the assessment of segmentation performance across the full spectrum of skin lesions.
